text_preview: This study proposes a novel “active core-skeleton-shell” configuration (lipase@MPHM) featuring enzyme cores, MOF skeletons, and PAA shells. The MOF skeleton maintains enzyme tertiary structure through coordinated interfacial hydrophobic interactions, while the PAA shell reinforces mechanical stability via electrostatic shielding at the MOFpolymer interface. Dual-interfacial design enables unprecedented enzyme stability against extreme pH (1−14), chemical denaturants (urea/DMSO), and prolonged storage (>11 weeks), outperforming conventional lipase@MOF systems in both catalytic efficiency (596% enhancement) and reusability. The work establishes a generalizable interface engineering paradigm for developing robust biohybrid materials in biocatalysis and beyond. 2. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

section: 3.2.1.2. Reaction solution pH. The pH of the reaction solution is an important factor affecting the catalytic activity of the lipase, which is often reduced by altering the charge balance of the lipase active site [17]. According to Fig. $^ { 6 , }$ in the pH range of 6 to 8, the relative activity reaches its maximum at pH 7, which generates a large decrease in the relative activity with either increasing or decreasing pH. An overly acidic or alkaline environment can affect the spatial conformation of the lipase molecule or even inactivate it. At the same time, the $\mathrm { U i O } { \cdot } 6 6 { \cdot } \mathrm { N H } _ { 2 }$ carrier itself is acidic, which makes it difficult to maintain the activity of the lipase molecules when they are bound to the carrier matrix by the impact of Lewis acid sites. Therefore, the immobilized lipase exhibited the highest activity at reaction solution of $^ { \cdot } { } _ { 7 , \cdot }$ which has been reported to confirm this. Kuang et al. [10] showed that Aspergillus oryzae lipase immobilized on $\mathrm { U i O - } 6 6 { \mathrm { - } } \mathrm { N H _ { 2 } { \mathrm { - } } O A }$ substrate showed the maximum recovery of lipase activity (510%) at pH 7.0. Saadiah et al. [18] tested ROL at different pH conditions Specific lipase activity and activity recovery of ROL immobilized on hybridized dobby carbon nanotubes, it was observed that both activity recovery and specific lipase activity were maximum at pH 7.0.

text_preview: Efficient, green and stable catalysis has always been the core concept of enzyme catalysis in industrial processes for manufacturing. Therefore, we construct a new strategy with photothermal interfacial molecular transfer for green and efficient biodiesel catalysis. We encapsulate Candida albicans lipase B (CalB) in a γ-cyclodextrin metalorganic framework (γ-CD-MOF) loading with Ti3C2Tx by in situ growth and electrostatic assembly. The γ-CD-MOF not only protects the fragile enzyme, but also enhances the catalytic performance through the synergistic effects of porous adsorption (MOF pore structure) and interfacial enrichment (cyclodextrins host-guest assembly structure) for accelerating substrate transfer (642.6 %). The CalB@γ-CD-MOF/MXene-i activity can be regulated up to 274.6 % by exposure to near-infrared (NIR). Importantly, CalB@γ-CD-MOF/MXene-i achieves 93.3 % biodiesel conversio...

text_preview: Abstract: A hierarchical mesoporous zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) was processed based on cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) as a morphological regulating agent and amino acid (L-histidine) as assisting template agent. Burkholderia cepacia lipase (BCL) was successfully immobilized by ZIF-8 as the carrier via an adsorption method (BCL-ZIF-8). The immobilized lipase (BCL) showed utmost activity recovery up to 1279%, a 12-fold boost in its free counterpart. BCL-ZIF-8 was used as a biocatalyst in the transesterification reaction for the production of biodiesel with 93.4% yield. There was no significant lowering of conversion yield relative to original activity for BCL-ZIF-8 when continuously reused for eight cycles.
